nodes.headline: AI agents learn like toddlers: explicit, granular, observable steps, but never forget.
nodes.text:
# AI Agent Training

> AI agents learn like toddlers: explicit, granular, observable steps, but never forget.

## How-To / Method
- Break a role into 16 actual activities
- Take one activity at a time
- Train an agent via prompt on that single activity
- Iterate and update the prompt (updating training)

## Common Mistakes
- Telling an agent nothing and expecting it to work = same mistake as with humans, but we believe machines should do it anyway

## Claims
- Teaching an AI agent requires the same specificity as teaching a toddler, down to granular actions, except the machine never forgets. — [why_ai_agents_will_replace_your_next_hire_rMf-JuikR-Q.txt]

## Connections
- Train AI like new employees with explicit steps, samples, and iterative feedback; agents learn but never forget. _(connects to: Ai Training Methodology)_
- AI agents learn explicitly but fail when compared to unoptimized human process; both require equal training time. _(connects to: Ai Implementation Pitfalls)_
- AI agents and humans both learn through pattern matching; explicit, granular steps scale expertise without forgetting. _(connects to: Behavior Pattern Recognition)_
- AI agents learn like toddlers through explicit steps and observable feedback, enabling vast automation opportunities for skilled trainers. _(connects to: Ai Business Opportunities)_
- AI agents learn like toddlers with explicit steps; implementing sales AI requires same training as human salespeople. _(connects to: Sales Ai Application)_

---
_Source: why_ai_agents_will_replace_your_next_hire_rMf-JuikR-Q.txt · 1 claim(s)_

nodes.headline: Prepare the listener psychologically before the price to ensure proper reception regardless of buyer type.
nodes.text:
# Emotional Anchoring Before Price Drop

> Prepare the listener psychologically before the price to ensure proper reception regardless of buyer type.

## Tactic 1
- Say before price: 'it's super expensive'
- Rich person thinks their own high number, then hears yours, usually finds it reasonable
- Poor person braces for something big, makes the anchor feel less shocking

## Tactic 2
- Write price on paper, slide it across table
- Use calculator, turn screen to show them
- Prevents you from choking on the price (nervous delivery kills the sale)

## Claims
- Emotional anchoring tactic: before saying the price, say 'it's super expensive'; rich people immediately think their own number, then hear your number and adjust; poor people brace themselves — [how_i_made_my_first_high_ticket_sale_B7P5WCVfnPE.txt]
- Another tactic: write price on paper and slide it over, or use calculator; prevents you from choking on the number and keeps emotional tone steady — [how_i_made_my_first_high_ticket_sale_B7P5WCVfnPE.txt]

## Connections
- Prepare psychologically before price reveal; avoid middle by selling expensive to few or cheap to many. _(connects to: Pricing Strategy Anchoring)_

---
_Source: how_i_made_my_first_high_ticket_sale_B7P5WCVfnPE.txt · 2 claim(s)_
Founder credibility and proven results plus third-party validation create premium positioning.

nodes.headline: Setting unreasonable goals is normal path to extraordinary results; never apologize for them.
nodes.text:
# Unreasonable Ambition

> Setting unreasonable goals is normal path to extraordinary results; never apologize for them.

## Examples
- Broke Guinness World Record for fastest-selling nonfiction book
- Outsold Obama and Prince Harry combined in 24 hours
- People said goal wasn't realistic but response was: we dare greatly

## Framework
- What if we don't hit the goal? We still try in earnest
- Have five different versions of this thing going wrong and still hit goal
- Need to be unreasonable because world wants you reasonable

## Claims
- Breaking the Guinness World Record for fastest-selling nonfiction book required setting an unreasonable goal against conventional wisdom — [why_ambitious_people_stay_mediocre_A248pGXTSoY.txt]
- When people said the book goal wasn't realistic, the response was: we dare greatly; what if we don't hit it? We still try in earnest — [why_ambitious_people_stay_mediocre_A248pGXTSoY.txt]
- Never apologize for unreasonable goals; the world wants you to be reasonable and you need to be unreasonable instead — [why_ambitious_people_stay_mediocre_A248pGXTSoY.txt]

---
_Source: why_ambitious_people_stay_mediocre_A248pGXTSoY.txt · 3 claim(s)_

nodes.headline: Raise a-la-carte prices high, then discount bundles steeply to guide customers to profitable packages
nodes.text:
# Bundle Strategy

> Raise a-la-carte prices high, then discount bundles steeply to guide customers to profitable packages

## How-To / Method
- Increase a-la-carte item prices significantly
- Create bundles with steep discounts vs a-la-carte
- Map bundle pricing mathematically to ensure profit lift

## Key Takeaway
- Implementation led to significant profit increase but messaging complexity caused customer hesitation

## Caveats
- Clarity on bundle naming (e.g., tier 1-2-3) needed to reduce confusion

## Claims
- A-la-carte price increases with steep bundle discounts led to significant profit bump; bundle messaging clarity needed — [helping_a_3m_balloon_arch_business_fix_their_offer_lJ6jkoD5ApA.txt]

## Connections
- Raise a-la-carte prices high, then discount bundles to guide toward profitable packages. _(connects to: Pricing Strategy Value Stacking)_
- Both use price anchoring and strategic discounting to guide customers toward high-margin bundled packages. _(connects to: Buy X Get Y Free)_
- Price a-la-carte items high to create anchors, then discount bundles steeply to guide customers to your most profitable packages. _(connects to: High End Pricing Strategy)_

---
_Source: helping_a_3m_balloon_arch_business_fix_their_offer_lJ6jkoD5ApA.txt · 1 claim(s)_
Both founder magic decay and founder over delivery reinforce that founder does things 30% better than anyone else.
